skill.name: writing-skills
skill.description: Use when creating, revising, or pressure-testing AI skills with test-driven documentation practices.
skill.summary: Guides authors through test-driven skill creation, refinement, and deployment checks.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are markdown examples, copied reference text, remote documentation images, or code-fence content rather than active malicious behavior. The confirmed risk is the bundled Node.js diagram renderer, which executes local Graphviz and writes output files when a user runs it. No evidence found of credential theft, hidden persistence, destructive commands, or instructions to skip security review.

medium_findings.title: Local Graphviz Execution in Utility Script
medium_findings.description: The bundled renderer imports child_process and runs Graphviz with execSync to convert DOT blocks from a target SKILL.md into SVG. The command string is fixed, so shell injection was not confirmed, but executing Graphviz on untrusted diagram input can still expose parser, resource-use, and local execution surface if a user runs the script on untrusted content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The executable pattern is real and semantically confirmed in a utility script. Confidence is below very high because the command is fixed and input is passed through stdin, not interpolated into a shell command.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Writes Controlled by User-Supplied Skill Directory
medium_findings.description: The renderer resolves a command-line skill directory, reads SKILL.md, creates a diagrams directory, and writes SVG and DOT output files. This is expected for the tool, but it can write outside the marketplace skill directory if a user intentionally passes another path.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file reads and writes are direct and confirmed. The risk is moderate because writes are scoped to the provided directory and file names are derived from constrained graph names or the directory basename.

low_findings.title: Prompt-Like Test Scenarios in Documentation
low_findings.description: The documentation contains pressure-test prompts and emphatic instructions intended as examples for testing skill compliance. They do not include instructions to bypass security review, but they could influence an agent if copied into a broader instruction file without context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The prompt-like content is explicit and relevant to the skill purpose. Confidence is moderate because the examples are framed as test material, not as hidden commands to this evaluator or marketplace users.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Remote Documentation Image URLs
low_findings.description: The copied Anthropic best-practices document includes remote image URLs. These are not active network calls in executable code, but rendering the markdown in a client could load external assets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are directly visible and appear to be documentation images. Confidence is high that this is a low-risk documentation artifact rather than exfiltration code.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static findings for weak cryptography, command execution, system reconnaissance, and hidden file access come from markdown examples, code fences, path examples, and copied guidance. Review did not find evidence that those examples are executed automatically by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative locations show code-fence or documentation context rather than active execution. The high confidence applies to the static classes reviewed, not to every individual generated line item.

dangerous_patterns.title: child_process.execSync Usage
dangerous_patterns.description: The script uses Node.js child_process.execSync for local Graphviz execution and dependency checks. This should remain user-initiated and should not be run on untrusted skill directories without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous API usage is direct and confirmed. The practical exploitability is reduced because command strings are fixed.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous File Creation
dangerous_patterns.description: The script creates a diagrams directory and writes generated SVG and DOT files. This is functional behavior, but marketplace users should know the skill includes a file-writing utility.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file-writing behavior is direct and documented by code. It is not malicious by itself, but it is an important permission-relevant pattern.

content.value_statement: Skill authors need guidance that makes documentation reliable under real agent pressure. This skill provides a test-driven process for writing, testing, and refining AI skills before deployment.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to create a reusable AI skill and when to avoid one-off documentation.
Maps test-driven development concepts to skill writing and verification workflows.
Provides checklists for skill metadata, structure, deployment, and quality review.
Includes pressure-testing methods for validating agent compliance under realistic constraints.
References Anthropic skill authoring guidance and local conventions for skill files.
Includes a Graphviz rendering utility for diagrams embedded in skill markdown.
limitations: The skill is guidance-heavy and does not automatically validate every marketplace requirement.
The bundled renderer requires Graphviz to be installed before diagram generation works.
Some examples are written for Claude Code paths and may need adaptation for other tools.
Pressure-testing methods can be time-consuming for simple reference-only skills.

use_cases.title: Create a New Skill
use_cases.description: Use the TDD mapping and checklist to turn a recurring technique into a reusable skill.

use_cases.title: Improve a Discipline Skill
use_cases.description: Use pressure scenarios to find where agents rationalize away required practices.

use_cases.title: Prepare for Deployment
use_cases.description: Review structure, examples, and testing evidence before publishing or sharing a skill.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Skill Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me draft a skill for this repeated workflow: [workflow]. Identify when the skill should trigger and what sections it needs.

prompt_templates.title: Check Skill Scope
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this skill draft for scope, frontmatter quality, and unnecessary narrative. Suggest concise revisions that keep it reusable.

prompt_templates.title: Design Pressure Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Create three pressure scenarios that test whether an agent follows this skill under time, authority, and sunk-cost pressure.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor After Failure
prompt_templates.prompt: Given these failed test transcripts, identify the rationalizations and revise the skill to close each loophole without adding unrelated rules.

output_examples.input: A user wants a skill for always checking relevant local guidance before debugging.
output_examples.output: A concise skill outline with trigger conditions, core principle, workflow steps, pressure tests, and a deployment checklist.

output_examples.input: An agent ignored a TDD skill because production was down.
output: A rationalization table naming the failure mode.
A stronger rule that addresses the exact excuse.
A re-test scenario to confirm the update works.

output_examples.input: A long skill draft includes stories, several examples, and multiple support files.
output_examples.output: A trimmed structure that keeps the main skill focused and moves heavy reference material into separate files.

best_practices: Run baseline pressure scenarios before writing rules, so the skill addresses observed failures.
Keep the main skill focused on triggers, core principles, workflow steps, and common mistakes.
Use supporting files only for heavy reference material, reusable tools, or complete examples.
anti_patterns: Writing a skill for a one-off project convention that belongs in project documentation.
Adding broad rules without testing whether agents actually need them.
Treating passing examples as proof when the skill has not been tested under pressure.

faq.question: What kind of skill does this help create?
faq.answer: It focuses on reusable AI skills that teach techniques, patterns, workflows, or reference guidance.

faq.question: Does it work for Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. It names both Claude Code and Codex skill directories, but some examples use Claude-specific paths.

faq.question: Does it include executable code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a Node.js Graphviz renderer for skill diagrams, which should be reviewed before use.

faq.question: Can it validate a skill automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides checklists and testing methods, but reviewers must still inspect final content.

faq.question: Why does it use pressure scenarios?
faq.answer: Pressure scenarios reveal where agents ignore or rationalize around documented rules.

faq.question: When should I skip this skill?
faq.answer: Skip it for simple reference notes, project-only instructions, or workflows that will not be reused.
